peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness, strong anti-elitism and participatory democracy values drive moderate support for citizen assemblies, but low agreeableness and trust in existing institutions temper enthusiasm.

Moderate support from high GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (66.89) and populist people-centrism (64.85), tempered by very low openness (9.35) and a preference for established authority structures.
High openness and participatory democracy preference (60.78) drive moderate support for citizen assemblies, but low trust in institutions tempers enthusiasm.
Moderate support from high GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (64.58) and free-spirit autonomy (66.71), tempered by very low agreeableness (16.2) and skepticism of collective deliberation.

Low populist attitudes (people-centrism 12%) and very low GAL participatory democracy preference (9%) blunt enthusiasm for citizen assemblies, but strong anti-elitism subcomponent (12%) and moderate distrust of existing institutions (perceived legitimacy 36%) leave room for cautious openness.
Moderate support driven by high extraversion and people-centrism but tempered by low openness to new experiences and a preference for traditional top-down structures.

Very low openness and extremely low agreeableness suggest impatience with group deliberation, while high libertarian civil-liberties scores might endorse the idea in abstract, but the concrete mechanism feels slow and untrustworthy to this profile.

Moderate support from high participatory democracy preference (55.52) and civic engagement (active), tempered by very low openness (19.77) and low trust in institutions (20.09) making full delegation uncertain.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High system justification and respect for legitimate authority uphold losing consent, while low agreeableness and occasional civic participation make it a pragmatic rather than passionate stance.

Clear support driven by high civic nationalism (95.25) and trust in electoral process (91.23), but moderated by strong authoritarian submission (8.78) and a Manichean worldview (93.0) that may question opponents' legitimacy.
Strong fairness/equity values (0.75) and high internal political efficacy (89.34) support loser consent, though high collective narcissism (89.11) introduces some resistance.
Weak support due to low political trust (external efficacy 18.45) and high system justification (92.88) creating a grudging acceptance of procedural norms despite low faith in fairness.

High system justification (political 86%) and authoritarian conformity (68%) support acceptance of electoral outcomes, yet low internal efficacy (9%) and dismissive-avoidant attachment reduce emotional investment in consent.
Mild opposition reflecting high social dominance orientation and low agreeableness (HEXACO), combined with a populist 'people vs. elite' worldview that questions legitimacy of losing.

High system justification and strong authoritarian law-and-order orientation support acceptance of outcomes, but very low agreeableness and a competitive-jungle worldview make the person resentful of losing and reluctant to extend legitimacy to rivals.

Clear support driven by high agreeableness (57.5) and fairness/equity values, but moderated by high collective narcissism (78.08) and authoritarian submission (89.94) that may resist accepting loss to outgroups.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong authoritarian submission, conventionalism, and high conscientiousness solidly support peaceful transfers as a rule-of-order norm.

Strong support from high trust in institutions (84.51), civic nationalism (95.25), and respect for tradition (55.7), though a history of justice system contact and moderate dangerous worldview (66.84) prevent maximal endorsement.
High dutifulness (96.25) and moderate trust in government (67.99) support peaceful transfers, but a history of domestic violence exposure and criminal record create slight ambivalence.
Strong support driven by high HEXACO agreeableness (84.26) and low authoritarian aggression (13.45), combined with a personal history of violence exposure that makes stability appealing.

Strong law-and-order preference (73%), high fearfulness (90%), and conventionalism (15%) create a firm baseline for orderly transfers, though past exposure to law-enforcement violence (witness, chronic) slightly tempers idealization.
Near-neutral, as high authoritarianism and low conscientiousness create ambivalence toward procedural norms, while high libertarianism and distrust in government pull in opposing directions.

Very high conscientiousness combined with strong authority-submission and system-justification scores creates a firm rule-following preference for procedural continuity even when the winner is disliked.
Strong support from high care/harm foundation (89.14) and low openness to violence (rejection of political violence is high), though high authoritarian aggression (82.43) creates slight tension.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high libertarian skepticism of state control and strong preference for personal autonomy oppose compulsory voting despite moderate civic engagement tendencies.

Clear opposition due to very high libertarian civil liberties support (96.81) and anti-state-control skepticism (80.8), combined with very low voting participation (1.62) and a preference for personal autonomy.
Libertarian anti-authoritarian leanings (74.11) and high autonomy (87.87) oppose compulsory voting, yet high civic nationalism (60.98) and people-centrism (65.15) pull toward mild support.
Clear opposition from libertarian anti-state-control orientation (56.81) and very low political participation (voting 13.55), viewing compulsion as coercive and ineffective.

High GAL-TAN restrictionist outgroup policy (92%) and authoritarian support for coercive control (72%) lean toward mandating participation, but low voting participation (54%) and very low self-directedness (autonomy 90% free spirit) conflict with compulsion.
Clear support from high authoritarian submission and strong preference for social order, moderated by a libertarian mistrust of state compulsion.

Libertarian anti-state-control scores are extremely high (96th percentile), opposing mandated participation, yet high dutifulness and civic-nationalism pull toward supporting the duty to vote, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence.

Mild opposition due to very low libertarianism (0.69) and high authoritarian submission (89.94) which normally favor state mandates, but low political efficacy (45.01) and very low voting participation (15.54) reduce enthusiasm.
